pr.title: Implement "code bytes to go for next trophy"
pr.author: ribbanya

summary: Added a progress-reporting line to tools/project.py that tells contributors how many code bytes remain until the next fancy progress item/trophy. The calculation reuses the already-displayed floored code trophy count, derives bytes per fancy fraction from code_total / code_fancy_frac, rounds the next threshold up with math.ceil, and subtracts current code_progress. The PR also includes small Python formatting/import cleanup in the same file.
what_changed: In print_category, introduced code_items = math.floor(code_frac * unit.code_fancy_frac) and reused it in the existing fancy progress message.
When config.progress_use_fancy is enabled, calculates bytes_per_frac as unit.code_total / unit.code_fancy_frac.
Calculates the next code trophy threshold with math.ceil((code_items + 1) * bytes_per_frac).
Calculates bytes_to_go as bytes_next - unit.code_progress and prints: "Code bytes to go for next trophy: {bytes_to_go}".
Consolidated typing imports into one line including Any, Dict, List, Optional, Set, Tuple, and Union.
Applied small formatting changes around Optional[Path] assignment and a conditional implicit= argument.
smart_moves: Reused the same floored code_items value for both the displayed current trophy count and the next-threshold calculation, avoiding subtle disagreement between the two outputs.
Used math.ceil for the next byte threshold so fractional trophy boundaries round up to a concrete byte target.
Scoped the new output under config.progress_use_fancy, so the extra trophy-specific line only appears when the fancy progress system is active.
Computed the target from unit.code_total and unit.code_fancy_frac rather than hard-coding trophy sizes, preserving compatibility with configurable fancy progress fractions.
decomp_lessons: Progress tooling can improve contributor motivation by translating raw code byte progress into the exact number of bytes needed for the next visible milestone.
When adding progress metrics, derive them from existing ProgressUnit fields instead of duplicating constants or assumptions from progress.json.
If a displayed progress count is rounded or floored, store that rounded value in a local variable before using it for related calculations; this keeps user-facing messages internally consistent.
Small developer-experience changes in tooling should stay localized and avoid touching decompilation source files when the behavior is purely reporting.
naming_conventions: Used descriptive snake_case locals: code_items, bytes_per_frac, bytes_next, and bytes_to_go.
Kept existing ProgressUnit-style field names such as code_progress, code_total, code_fancy_frac, code_fancy_item, data_progress, and data_total.
The printed phrase uses the user-facing term "trophy" for the next fancy code milestone.
review_feedback: No issue comments, review comments, or reviews were present in the PR slice.
searchable_terms: code bytes to go for next trophy
follow_up_queries: Check whether the output behaves sensibly at 100% code progress, where code_items may already equal code_fancy_frac.
Search later PRs for refinements to "Code bytes to go for next trophy" or changes to progress_use_fancy output.
Verify whether tools/project.py retains a valid math import after the import cleanup shown in this diff.
